name: Tyrannosaurus Rex
summary: Tyrannosaurus Rex was one of the largest land predators ever to walk the Earth, reaching up to 40 feet long and weighing over 8 tons. It had tiny arms but enormously powerful jaws capable of crushing bone. T. Rex lived in what is now western North America about 68 to 66 million years ago.
fun_fact: T. Rex could eat up to 500 pounds of meat in a single bite!

name: Triceratops
summary: Triceratops was a large horned dinosaur with three facial horns and a bony neck frill, making it one of the most recognizable dinosaurs. It could grow up to 30 feet long and weigh around 12 tons. Triceratops grazed on low-lying plants and likely lived in herds for protection.
fun_fact: Triceratops had one of the largest skulls of any land animal ever — up to 8 feet long!

name: Ankylosaurus
summary: Ankylosaurus was a heavily armored dinosaur covered in thick bony plates and spikes, with a massive club at the end of its tail it could swing to defend itself. It was about 20 to 26 feet long and was built low to the ground. Despite its fearsome appearance, it was a peaceful plant-eater.
fun_fact: The tail club of Ankylosaurus was so powerful it could break the bones of a T. Rex!

name: Spinosaurus
summary: Spinosaurus was the largest carnivorous dinosaur known, even bigger than T. Rex, reaching up to 46 feet in length. It had a distinctive tall sail on its back and was well-adapted to hunting fish in rivers and shallow waters. Scientists believe it spent much of its time in or near water.
fun_fact: Spinosaurus is the only known dinosaur adapted for swimming — its bones were dense like a crocodile's!

name: Iguanodon
summary: Iguanodon was a large plant-eating dinosaur that could walk on two or four legs, giving it great flexibility in feeding and moving. It had a distinctive spike on each thumb, possibly used for defense or foraging. Iguanodon was among the first dinosaurs ever to be scientifically described.
fun_fact: When Iguanodon was first discovered in 1822, scientists thought its thumb spike was a horn on its nose!

name: Parasaurolophus
summary: Parasaurolophus was a duck-billed dinosaur famous for its long, hollow, tube-like crest extending back from its skull. Scientists believe it used this crest to produce loud, resonant calls to communicate with others in its herd. It was a social animal that moved in large groups.
fun_fact: The hollow crest of Parasaurolophus could produce sounds like a trombone or foghorn!

name: Mosasaurus
summary: Mosasaurus was a massive marine reptile that ruled the seas of the Late Cretaceous, growing up to 56 feet long. It was a powerful swimmer with a long, flattened tail and paddle-like limbs, hunting fish, sharks, and even other mosasaurs. It was at the very top of the ocean food chain.
fun_fact: Mosasaurus could unhinge its jaw like a snake to swallow very large prey whole!

name: Elasmosaurus
summary: Elasmosaurus was a long-necked plesiosaur with a neck so long it made up half of its total 46-foot body length. It used its tiny head and sharp teeth to snatch fish swimming beneath it. It lived in the vast shallow sea that once covered the middle of North America.
fun_fact: Elasmosaurus had 72 neck vertebrae — more than any other known animal!

name: Kronosaurus
summary: Kronosaurus was a short-necked plesiosaur and one of the largest marine reptiles of the Cretaceous, reaching up to 36 feet long. It had an enormous head armed with large, cone-shaped teeth perfect for crushing shells and bones. It hunted plesiosaurs, giant turtles, and large fish.
fun_fact: Kronosaurus's head alone was nearly 10 feet long — about the size of a small car!

name: Pteranodon
summary: Pteranodon was a large flying reptile with a wingspan of up to 23 feet and a distinctive backward-pointing crest on its head. It soared over the oceans, diving to catch fish much like a modern pelican. Despite its dramatic appearance, Pteranodon had no teeth — just a long, pointed beak.
fun_fact: Pteranodon's crest may have been used like a rudder to help it steer while flying!

name: Quetzalcoatlus
summary: Quetzalcoatlus was the largest flying animal ever known, with a wingspan of up to 36 feet — as wide as a small aircraft. When standing on the ground it was as tall as a giraffe. Scientists believe it could fly thousands of miles and may have stalked prey on the ground like a giant stork.
fun_fact: Quetzalcoatlus could launch itself into flight by pole-vaulting off all four limbs at once!

name: Tupuxuara
summary: Tupuxuara was a large pterosaur from Brazil with a rounded, fan-shaped head crest and a wingspan of up to 18 feet. It likely fed on fish along coastal areas and riverways. Its striking crest may have been used for display or to help regulate body temperature.
fun_fact: Tupuxuara's name comes from a Tupi word meaning 'familiar spirit' from Indigenous Brazilian mythology!

name: Hesperornithes
summary: Hesperornithes were large, flightless diving birds that lived during the Late Cretaceous, resembling a mix between a modern loon and a penguin. They were powerful swimmers with strong legs set far back on their body, and unlike most modern birds, they still had teeth. They dove for fish in shallow inland seas.
fun_fact: Hesperornithes were so adapted to swimming that they could barely walk on land — just like modern penguins!

name: Ichthyornis
summary: Ichthyornis was a seabird about the size of a modern tern that could truly fly, making it one of the most bird-like creatures of the Cretaceous. It still retained small teeth in its beak, a trait inherited from its dinosaur ancestors. It likely soared over the ancient sea and plucked fish from the surface.
fun_fact: Ichthyornis is a critical 'missing link' — it had a fully modern bird brain but still had a dinosaur's teeth!

name: Vegavis
summary: Vegavis was a duck-like bird that lived in Antarctica during the Late Cretaceous, proving that modern bird groups existed alongside the last of the non-avian dinosaurs. It was a waterfowl similar to modern ducks and geese, living near the shores of ancient Antarctic waterways. It survived the mass extinction that wiped out the non-avian dinosaurs.
fun_fact: A fossilized Vegavis syrinx (voice box) let scientists confirm it could quack — the oldest known bird call!

name: Repenomamus
summary: Repenomamus was one of the largest mammals of the Mesozoic Era, about the size of a Virginia opossum, which was huge for a Cretaceous mammal. Unlike most mammals of its time that were small and timid, it was a bold predator known to eat small dinosaurs. A fossil was found with baby dinosaur bones in its stomach.
fun_fact: Repenomamus is the only known Mesozoic mammal proven to have eaten dinosaurs — the tables were turned!

name: Zalambdalestes
summary: Zalambdalestes was a small, shrew-like placental mammal from Late Cretaceous Mongolia, thought to be a relative of modern rabbits and rodents. It had long hind legs built for jumping, pointed teeth for catching insects, and large eyes suited for a nocturnal lifestyle. It is one of the earliest known placental mammals.
fun_fact: Zalambdalestes may be a distant ancestor of all modern placental mammals, including humans!

name: Alphadon
summary: Alphadon was a small opossum-like marsupial mammal that scurried through the undergrowth of Late Cretaceous North America. It was about the size of a mouse and likely fed on insects, fruits, and eggs. As an early marsupial, it carried its young in a pouch, just like modern opossums and kangaroos.
fun_fact: Alphadon is one of the earliest known marsupials and is a distant relative of today's opossums!

name: Velociraptor
summary: Velociraptor was a small, agile predator about the size of a turkey, covered in feathers much like a modern bird. It had a large sickle-shaped claw on each foot used to pin down prey, and was likely an intelligent pack hunter. Despite its movie fame, the real Velociraptor was much smaller than depicted in films.
fun_fact: Real Velociraptors were about the size of a turkey — the 'raptors' in Jurassic Park were actually based on Deinonychus!

name: Pachycephalosaurus
summary: Pachycephalosaurus was a bipedal dinosaur famous for its dome-shaped skull up to 10 inches thick, which it likely used in head-butting contests with rivals — similar to bighorn sheep today. It was about 15 feet long and lived in the forests of Late Cretaceous North America. Its name literally means 'thick-headed lizard.'
fun_fact: Pachycephalosaurus's skull dome was so thick that it could withstand enormous impacts — nature's built-in crash helmet!

name: Argentinosaurus
summary: Argentinosaurus is one of the largest dinosaurs ever discovered, a titanosaur sauropod estimated to have been up to 100 feet long and weighed around 70 tons. It used its incredibly long neck to reach vegetation at many heights without moving. Found in Argentina, it may be the heaviest land animal that ever lived.
fun_fact: Each vertebra (backbone) of Argentinosaurus was over 5 feet tall — bigger than most humans!

name: Giganotosaurus
summary: Giganotosaurus was one of the largest meat-eating dinosaurs ever, rivaling T. Rex in size at around 40 to 43 feet long. It lived in South America and likely hunted the giant titanosaur sauropods like Argentinosaurus. It had a brain shaped like a banana — small, but tuned for hunting.
fun_fact: Giganotosaurus and T. Rex never met — they lived on different continents millions of years apart!

name: Therizinosaurus
summary: Therizinosaurus was a bizarre-looking dinosaur with enormous claws up to 3 feet long — the longest claws of any known animal. Despite its terrifying appearance, it was a plant-eater, likely using those claws to pull down branches. It stood around 16 feet tall and walked upright on two legs.
fun_fact: Therizinosaurus had the longest claws of any animal ever discovered — longer than a samurai sword!

name: Carnotaurus
summary: Carnotaurus was a large, fast-running predator with two distinctive bull-like horns above its eyes — the only large meat-eating dinosaur known to have horns. It had extremely small arms, even tinier than T. Rex's, and very powerful legs built for speed. It lived in Patagonia and hunted mid-sized prey.
fun_fact: Carnotaurus may have been one of the fastest large predatory dinosaurs, possibly reaching 35 mph!

name: Oviraptor
summary: Oviraptor was a feathered, bird-like dinosaur with a distinctive parrot-like beak and a crest on its head. Its name means 'egg thief,' but scientists now believe it was actually brooding its own eggs when first discovered — it was a caring parent, not a thief. It was likely omnivorous, eating plants, eggs, and small animals.
fun_fact: Oviraptor was wrongly accused of stealing eggs for 70 years — the eggs it was found with were actually its own!
